pamphlets.title: Relative clauses in Phi
pamphlets.summary: The whole description before the noun: pre-nominal relative clauses from first principles to nested patterns, the errors English pulls you toward, and exercises with a full answer key.

pamphlets.title: Complementizers and embedded clauses in Phi
pamphlets.summary: Thoughts within thoughts: the three opener–closer pairs — statements, questions, quotations — why the closers exist, and enough practice to make them reflex.

pamphlets.title: Evidentiality in Phi
pamphlets.summary: Four particles for claimed source: direct perception, inference, report, and assumption. Practice moves from the snake at the well through questions, retelling, and an optional source journal.

pamphlets.title: Counting in Phi: the ternary numerals and the four natures
pamphlets.summary: Three number-words, four group-words, four kinds of being — counting drilled to reflex, and then the harder skill: the honest about, where the sentence gets shorter as it gets truer.

pamphlets.title: How Phi names people
pamphlets.summary: A name is a word someone carries: ne the spoken capital, kona the raised hand, three honorifics that announce relationship rather than rank — and the family register, where a name at rest is proof of presence.

pamphlets.title: Punctuation you can hear
pamphlets.summary: Core Phi writes one mark and lexicalizes the rest: wa the question, shola and sholo the quotation boundary, kona the address, ne the name, and a dictation practice whose limits are stated explicitly.

pamphlets.title: Source material beside Phi
pamphlets.summary: How Phi uses vocabulary, transparent expressions, modules, and productive names while exact records, scripts, notation, testimony, and quotations remain in their own medium.

pamphlets.title: The three slots
pamphlets.summary: The whole grammar is thirty-five small words that never change: the frame, the stack, and the word's dress, drilled to reflex — with the interaction tables, the ruled readings, and the evening question where every particle costs what it claims.

pamphlets.title: How Phi is written in Tengwar
pamphlets.summary: A second hand for the same language: the fifteen consonant tengwar, the vowel tehtar that ride above and below them, and the one true invention — a hiatus rule that needs no vowel carrier at all, because Phi's own sound rules never leave it needing one.
